title: Looking for Condensed Gluons: A Cross-Scale Journey from the Deep Structure of Protons to High-Energy Cosmic Rays -- A Mini-Review
authors: Wei Zhu, Yu-Chen Tang, Ye-Yin Zhao, Bo Yang, Yu-Chen Xiong
abstract: Quark-gluon dynamics within protons and high-energy radiation phenomena in the universe are typically regarded as two entirely distinct fields. This paper aims to demonstrate that gluon condensation (GC) may serve as a direct bridge between these two fields. We review three key aspects of GC research: first, the Zhu-Shen-Ruan (ZSR) equation, as a nonlinear evolution equation based on structural symmetry, exhibits self-consistent connections with the DGLAP, BFKL and GLR-MQ-ZRS equations, providing a theoretical foundation for the generation of GC; second, the chaotic solutions and the shadowing-antishadowing synergy inherent in this equation can drive gluons to aggregate near the critical momentum, thereby forming a novel type of high-density, strongly interacting matter; third, these changes in microstructure manifest themselves as a broken-power-law feature in high-energy cosmic gamma-ray spectra, thereby offering new insights into the hadronic scenarios underlying certain astrophysical sources. Consequently, GC not only concerns the novel behaviour of quantum chromodynamics under extreme conditions but may also serve as a vital window for probing the deep structure of protons using cosmic-ray signals. With the advancement of higher-precision gamma-ray observations, hadron collision experiments and related theoretical research, the physical picture of GC and its observational criteria are expected to undergo more rigorous testing. Should this picture be confirmed, certain features in the high-energy gamma-ray spectrum will need to be re-examined within the deeper context of hadronic dynamics; simultaneously, GC may also provide a new entry point for research into pion condensation in nuclear physics and even condensed matter physics. Consequently, the significance of the search for GC extends beyond the model itself, reaching into multiple fields of natural science.
summary: This review explores the concept of Gluon Condensation (GC) as a link between proton structure and cosmic-ray physics. It argues that nonlinear QCD dynamics can drive gluons to aggregate, creating a high-density state that leaves detectable signatures in the gamma-ray spectra of astrophysical sources. If confirmed, this would provide a new way to probe the deep structure of hadrons using cosmic-ray signals.
main_problem: Investigating whether gluon condensation (GC) serves as a bridge between the microscopic internal structure of protons and macroscopic high-energy cosmic-ray phenomena.
main_result: The paper proposes that the ZSR nonlinear evolution equation predicts a gluon condensation state driven by the synergy of shadowing and antishadowing, which manifests as a broken-power-law feature in cosmic gamma-ray spectra.
method: The authors use Time-Ordered Perturbative Theory (TOPT) to derive the ZSR equation and apply the resulting GC spectrum model to fit observational data from gamma-ray observatories like LHAASO and Fermi-LAT.
model_or_system: The physical system involves quark-gluon dynamics within protons and high-energy astrophysical environments (e.g., SNRs, pulsars). The theoretical framework consists of a closed loop of QCD evolution equations (DGLAP, BFKL, GLR-MQ-ZRS, and ZSR).
key_observables: High-energy cosmic gamma-ray spectral energy distributions (SED), specifically broken-power-law features and the presence/absence of the pion-decay bump.
important_parameters: Critical momentum (kc), critical Bjorken-x (xc), gluon density, and the coupling constant (alpha_s).
main_assumptions: The derivation uses a cylindrically symmetric approximation and neglects certain higher-order QCD corrections; it also assumes a pion saturation mechanism.
figures_summary: Figure 1 compares CGC and GC scales; Figure 2 shows gluon distribution scales; Figure 3 illustrates the evolutionary path of QCD equations; Figure 4 shows elemental amplitudes; Figures 5-12 show fits of the GC model to various gamma-ray sources like SS 433 and J1534-571.
paper_structure: The paper is structured as a mini-review: first establishing the theoretical derivation of the ZSR equation and its role in QCD evolution; second, describing the mechanism of gluon condensation via chaos and shadowing/antishadowing; third, applying this model to astrophysical gamma-ray spectra; and finally, discussing implications for future collider and observatory testing.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Schematic diagrams of CGC (a,b) and GC (c,d). The GC threshold k2 c ≃CGC scale Q2 s, but xc ≪xs.
FIG. 2. Schematic diagrams of gluon distributions at different density scales.

FIG. 3. Evolution of the evolution equations beginning with the DGLAP equation (a) and they lead to (b) the BFKL equation, (c) the GLR-MQ-ZRS equation and (d) the ZSR equation. The dashed line denotes a virtual current probing the gluon distribution. Note that the four evolution equations form a closed circuit, which implies consistency among the four evolution equations.
FIG. 4. The elemental amplitudes for the four evolution equations based on Fig. 3.

FIG. 5. Spectral energy distribution (SED) of SS 433 measured by LHAASO [48] and comparison with the GC spectra (solid curve). Cγ = 5.33 × 10−15(GeV−2cm−2s−1), βγ = 1.79, βp = 0.80, EGC π = 1.0 TeV and Ecut π = 110 TeV. Different values of the average transverse momentum of pions allow the regions where the observation points deviate from the PL to be delineated by different lines, where ⟨pT ⟩= 0.2 GeV corresponds to the frozen region, i.e. the condition required by the GC spectrum: the average transverse momentum of pions can be neglected, and the pion yield shows saturation.
